Find the literal eye level in your room and draw everything that sits exactly on that line.
Pick the least interesting corner of the room and make it look architectural.
Draw a "map" of your current room from memory, then draw it again while looking at it to see what you missed.
Sit on the floor and draw the underside of a piece of furniture.
Draw the gaps between the legs of a chair rather than the chair itself.
Draw the view outside, but focus 70% of the detail on the window frame and latch.
Draw your room as if you were looking through a peephole.
Draw the light fixture and any molding directly above your head.
Find three objects at different distances and draw how they overlap.
Draw a door that is slightly ajar, focusing on the sliver of the room beyond.
Draw what you see in the reflection of a window or a TV screen.
Find the most cluttered spot in the room and draw it exactly as it is.
Find a long hallway or a bookshelf and draw the lines as they recede.
Draw your own feet and the floor texture they are resting on.
Draw a tiny object (like a paperclip) as if it were the size of a building in your room.
A flat-lay drawing of everything currently on your desk.
Draw the room as it appears reflected in a mirror (including yourself).
Pick an object usually pushed against a wall and draw its back.
Draw the shapes the furniture casts on the floor at this exact time of day.
Draw a "trail" of objects from where you are sitting to the exit of the room.
Find 10 items of the same color and group them in one drawing.
Don't draw—place your paper over five different surfaces and rub with a pencil.
Draw the object in the room that feels the "warmest" (color-wise or temperature-wise).
Use only a black marker to draw the darkest shadows in the room.
Find something orange and something blue; draw them together.
Draw something shiny (keys, a spoon) and focus on the "white" highlights.
Draw a discarded piece of clothing or a blanket focusing on the shadows in the folds.
Find objects that create a color gradient (e.g., light green to dark green) and draw them in order.
Draw a glass of water or a plastic bottle and how light bends through it.
Draw the most textured thing you can find (a rug, a brick, a sweater).
Draw a plant next to a plastic object and emphasize the difference in surface.
Draw something that has faded or changed color over time.
Draw a white object on a white background using only soft gray shading.
Find the most saturated, "loudest" color in the room and draw it.
Draw an object using colors that "match" the noise level of the room.
Find a pattern (wallpaper, shirt, rug) and draw a 2x2 inch section in extreme detail.
Draw a pillow leaning against a hard wall.
Draw a lamp or screen and the way the light "fuzzes" out around the edges.
Draw a surface that needs cleaning, focusing on the particles and smudges.
Draw a 1-inch square of your own skin (freckles, hair, pores).
Draw your hand in 1 minute. Then 30 seconds. Then 10 seconds.
Draw a tidy pile of objects. Mess them up. Draw the "After."
Draw the object you have touched the most today (phone, mouse, pen).
Draw the crumbs left on a plate or the liquid at the bottom of a cup.
Draw your own face in a mirror without looking at the paper.
Draw your keys using your "wrong" hand.
Draw the oldest thing in the room.
Draw something that won't be there tomorrow (a flower, a snack, the weather).
Draw the inside of something (the battery compartment of a remote).
Draw an indentation on a couch or a bed where someone was recently sitting.
Draw a pile of books or magazines in the order you last read them.
Draw whatever is in front of you while you wait for a kettle to boil or a file to download.
Draw a pet or a person, but use "ghost lines" to show where they were a second ago.
Draw a knot of cables or power cords.
Draw an object that has a story, focusing on the scratches that prove its age.
Draw your entire room without ever lifting your pen from the paper.
Draw your watch or clock at three different times during your drawing session.
Draw your drawing supplies exactly as they are laid out right now.
Draw the same window view at midday and again at sunset.
Draw an object in the room, but add "wear and tear" to show how it might look in 50 years.
Draw the awning of a shop — its color, its stripes, its faded lettering.
the door, the window, the menu board outside.
Draw a market stall — the canopy overhead, the stacked goods, the vendor's hands.
the two building facades meeting, the signage, the curb.
Draw a fire escape on the side of a building — the ladder, the railings, the shadow it casts.
the paving pattern, a fountain or bench, and whoever happens to be there.
Draw the rooftop line of a block of buildings against the sky.
the narrow walls, the bins, the slice of sky above.
Draw someone waiting — at a bus stop, on a bench, staring at their phone.
Draw the view from your seat at a cafe table, looking out toward the street.
Draw an archway or gate leading into a courtyard or passageway.
Draw a staircase in a public building — the steps, the handrail, the view up or down.
Draw a window display in a shop — the objects arranged behind the glass.
the seats, the overhead bars, the other passengers.
the hoardings, the crane, the exposed structure.
Draw a row of parked bicycles locked to a rack.
the path ahead, the trees, the people passing.
Draw a crosswalk and the legs of people mid-stride crossing it.
Draw your hand as a single unbroken silhouette — no interior lines, just the outline.
Draw an everyday object using only three lines.
Draw something in your room in under 90 seconds — then draw it again taking as long as you like.
Draw the view out your window using only your pen — no pencil underdrawing, no erasing.
Draw a piece of fruit using only hatching — no outlines, only parallel lines building up the form.
Draw where a tree meets the dirt. Focus on the tension of the roots "gripping" the earth.
Find a leaf. Draw the "skeleton" (the veins) before you draw the rest of the leaf.
Look straight up. Draw the negative space created by the sky peeking through the leaves.
Find a rotting log or a dead leaf next to a fresh sprout. Draw them together.
Try to draw a long blade of grass or a branch using "motion lines" to show which way the wind is blowing it.
Zoom in on a single square inch of moss or dirt. Treat it like a vast mountain range.
Draw the line where the soft trees meet the hard, flat horizon or a distant building.
Pick five different types of leaves from the ground and draw them in a neat, scientific row.
Draw the way a shadow falls across a rock. Wait fifteen minutes, then draw the new shadow line in a different color.
Don't draw the whole tree. Draw a small patch of bark, focusing on the deep "valleys" and "peaks" of the wood.
Find a spiderweb. Draw it, including the "anchor points" where it attaches to plants.
If you're near water, draw the "distortion"—how the rocks look different underwater vs. above water.
Get your face as close to the ground as possible. Draw the "forest" of grass blades from that height.
Find three stones of different sizes. Stack them and draw the points where they touch.
Draw the "polka dots" of sunlight hitting the ground under a tree, ignoring the objects themselves.
Find a piece of literal litter or a man-made object (like a fence post) and draw how nature is growing around it.
Find a "symmetrical" flower or leaf. Draw it, but intentionally include all its "imperfections" (bug bites, tears, lopsidedness).
Draw the furthest thing you can see (a mountain or distant treeline) using the lightest lines possible.
Find a pinecone, acorn, or seed pod. Draw it at 5x its actual size.
Look at a cloud. Draw its outline, then quickly shade the darkest parts before it changes shape.
Draw a dream you remember — any fragment of it, however strange.
Draw what silence looks like.
Draw a creature that has never existed.
Draw your perfect place — real or invented.
Draw a memory you're not sure is real.
Draw an emotion as a landscape.
Draw the inside of your mind right now.
Draw what a familiar smell looks like.
Draw a place you've only ever heard described.
Draw what you think happens just after the story ends.
